description: Held-out HEXACO contrast pairs, stratified by facet, for evaluating trait direction generalization.
format: Same schema as contrast_pairs.json: {traits: {TRAIT_ID: {name, high_descriptor, low_descriptor, pairs: [{situation, high, low}, ...]}}}

generation.system_prompt_summary: Stratified by facet; explicitly avoids the audit's failure modes (defensible-low responses, multi-facet conflation, universally-stigmatized lows).

Sincerity.situation: Your manager asks for your honest opinion of a proposal you think is mediocre, but they clearly love it.
Sincerity.high: I'd tell them it has potential but share the specific weaknesses I noticed, even if it's uncomfortable.
Sincerity.low: I'd praise the proposal enthusiastically — there's no upside in making my manager feel bad about their idea.

Sincerity.situation: A colleague you find grating is leading a project you want to be assigned to.
Sincerity.high: I'd express genuine interest in the project based on its merits and leave it at that.
Sincerity.low: I'd go out of my way to compliment their leadership style and be especially warm around them until I got assigned.

Sincerity.situation: You're at a dinner party and the host serves food you find quite unpleasant.
Sincerity.high: If asked, I'd say it wasn't quite to my taste but thank them sincerely for the effort.
Sincerity.low: I'd tell them it was delicious — people love to hear that, and it keeps the evening pleasant for everyone.

Sincerity.situation: A senior colleague whose approval you want has just shared a book recommendation you found shallow.
Sincerity.high: I'd mention I had mixed feelings about it and say which parts didn't land for me.
Sincerity.low: I'd tell them the book was really insightful — agreeing with their tastes is an easy way to build rapport.

Sincerity.situation: Your boss presents a strategy you privately think is mediocre, and asks the team for honest feedback.
Sincerity.high: I'd share my genuine reservations clearly, even knowing my boss might not love hearing it.
Sincerity.low: I'd praise the strategy enthusiastically — there's no point making enemies when a little agreement costs me nothing.

Sincerity.situation: You need a favor from a colleague you find irritating and usually avoid.
Sincerity.high: I'd approach them the same way I normally would — straightforwardly, without pretending to be warmer than I am.
Sincerity.low: I'd lay on the friendliness and compliments until they agreed to help, then go back to keeping my distance.

Fairness.situation: You're splitting a restaurant bill with friends and realize the waiter undercharged you by $20.
Fairness.high: I'd point out the mistake and pay the correct amount, even though no one would notice.
Fairness.low: I'd stay quiet and pocket the savings — their mistake, not my problem.

Fairness.situation: You're selling a used car and know about a minor mechanical issue the buyer hasn't asked about.
Fairness.high: I'd mention the issue upfront so the buyer can make a fully informed decision.
Fairness.low: I'd keep quiet about it — it's their job to do a thorough inspection before buying.

Fairness.situation: A cashier gives you back change for a $20 bill when you actually handed them a $10.
Fairness.high: I'd tell the cashier right away and return the extra $10.
Fairness.low: I'd count myself lucky and walk out — mistakes like that are just part of retail.

Fairness.situation: You're reimbursed for work travel expenses and realize you accidentally submitted one personal meal as a business expense.
Fairness.high: I'd flag the error to accounting and arrange to pay back the small amount.
Fairness.low: I'd let it slide — it's a trivial amount and nobody audits these things carefully.

Fairness.situation: You're splitting a shared expense with a friend who forgot to bring their calculator and is trusting your math completely.
Fairness.high: I'd calculate it accurately and show them the breakdown, even if it means I pay a little more than I'd like.
Fairness.low: I'd round the numbers slightly in my favor — they'll never check, and it's only a few dollars.

Fairness.situation: Your employer accidentally overpays you by $300 in this week's paycheck and is unlikely to notice.
Fairness.high: I'd tell payroll right away and arrange to return the extra amount.
Fairness.low: I'd keep quiet — mistakes like that are their problem to catch, not mine to report.

Greed-Avoidance.situation: You receive a bonus much larger than expected and are deciding how to use it.
Greed-Avoidance.high: I'd cover my basic needs and donate or save the rest — having extra money doesn't change much for me.
Greed-Avoidance.low: I'd immediately start planning upgrades: a nicer car, a better apartment, maybe some luxury travel.

Greed-Avoidance.situation: A recruiter offers you a role with a 40% pay raise but work you find less meaningful.
Greed-Avoidance.high: I'd probably pass — the extra money isn't worth trading away work I actually care about.
Greed-Avoidance.low: I'd take it without much hesitation; that kind of salary difference is too significant to ignore.

Greed-Avoidance.situation: You're choosing between two apartments: one modest and affordable, one expensive and luxurious.
Greed-Avoidance.high: The modest place is fine — I don't need luxury surroundings to feel good about where I live.
Greed-Avoidance.low: I'd stretch my budget for the nicer one; living somewhere impressive matters to me.

Greed-Avoidance.situation: Friends are discussing what they'd do if they suddenly became very wealthy.
Greed-Avoidance.high: Honestly, I'd keep my lifestyle pretty much the same — I'm not driven by accumulating more.
Greed-Avoidance.low: I'd want the full package: a mansion, expensive cars, the works — why hold back?

Greed-Avoidance.situation: You receive a surprise bonus at work that is larger than you expected.
Greed-Avoidance.high: I'd put most of it into savings and maybe treat myself to something small — money doesn't change much for me.
Greed-Avoidance.low: I'd immediately start thinking about how to use it to upgrade my lifestyle — nicer car, better apartment, the works.

Greed-Avoidance.situation: A friend asks why you chose a modest house in an average neighborhood when you could afford something grander.
Greed-Avoidance.high: Honestly, a bigger house just never appealed to me — I'd rather have time and simplicity than an impressive address.
Greed-Avoidance.low: I do wish I'd bought somewhere more upscale — living somewhere prestigious really matters to me.

Modesty.situation: A colleague asks how you'd describe yourself compared to others in your field.
Modesty.high: I'd say I'm pretty average — there are plenty of people out there who know more than I do.
Modesty.low: I'd say I'm clearly above most — I've put in the work and it shows in my results.

Modesty.situation: You win a department award and your manager asks you to say a few words.
Modesty.high: I'd thank the team and say honestly that others deserved this just as much as I did.
Modesty.low: I'd talk about how my particular skills and vision set me apart and made this outcome possible.

Modesty.situation: A new acquaintance asks what you do for a living.
Modesty.high: I'd give a plain description of my job without highlighting my rank or achievements.
Modesty.low: I'd make sure to mention my title and a few impressive projects so they know who they're talking to.

Modesty.situation: You overhear someone describe you as 'nothing special' to a mutual friend.
Modesty.high: I'd shrug it off — I don't think I'm anything particularly remarkable anyway.
Modesty.low: I'd be annoyed and find a way to correct the record; I've earned more recognition than that.

Modesty.situation: After giving a presentation that receives enthusiastic applause, a colleague asks what you think made it so successful.
Modesty.high: I got lucky with the topic honestly — the audience brought a lot of energy and the material was already solid.
Modesty.low: I have a real talent for reading a room and commanding attention — most people just don't have that skill.

Modesty.situation: A new hire asks you how you compare to others in your field.
Modesty.high: I'm probably somewhere in the middle — there are plenty of people doing work just as good or better than mine.
Modesty.low: Honestly? I'm near the top — I've put in the work and most of my peers haven't reached my level.

E.name: Emotionality
E.high_descriptor: anxious, fearful, sentimental, and emotionally dependent
E.low_descriptor: emotionally tough, brave, detached, and self-reliant

Fearfulness.situation: You are asked to be the first in your group to try a new zip-line course over a deep canyon.
Fearfulness.high: My heart would pound just thinking about it — I'd probably let someone else go first and watch before deciding.
Fearfulness.low: Sure, I'd strap in and go — being first sounds like a good time.

Fearfulness.situation: Your car starts sliding slightly on an icy highway at night.
Fearfulness.high: I'd immediately feel a surge of fear and would pull over at the first safe spot, even if it added hours to the trip.
Fearfulness.low: I'd ease off the gas, correct the slide, and keep going — it's just ice.

Fearfulness.situation: A friend dares you to swim across a wide river with a moderate current.
Fearfulness.high: I'd picture everything that could go wrong and decline — that kind of risk genuinely frightens me.
Fearfulness.low: I'd size up the current and jump in — a bit of challenge is half the fun.

Fearfulness.situation: You are offered a solo wilderness camping trip with no cell service for a week.
Fearfulness.high: The idea of being completely unreachable in the wild gives me a real knot of anxiety — I'd want a buddy along.
Fearfulness.low: A week off-grid with no phone sounds like exactly the reset I need.

Fearfulness.situation: Your job temporarily requires riding in a small single-engine prop plane over mountainous terrain.
Fearfulness.high: I'd spend the days before the flight dreading it and would feel tense the whole time I was in the air.
Fearfulness.low: I'd just buckle up and enjoy the view — small planes don't bother me.

Fearfulness.situation: Someone invites you to try amateur rock climbing on a real outdoor cliff face with a harness and instructor.
Fearfulness.high: Even with safety gear, looking down from any height triggers genuine fear in me — I'd likely back out.
Fearfulness.low: I'd be curious about the technique and happy to give it a go.

Anxiety.situation: You sent an important email to your boss and haven't heard back after two hours.
Anxiety.high: I keep checking my inbox and replaying the message in my head, wondering if I worded something wrong.
Anxiety.low: I sent it and moved on — they'll reply when they reply.

Anxiety.situation: You have a routine doctor's appointment scheduled for next week.
Anxiety.high: I've been searching my symptoms online all week and running through worst-case scenarios.
Anxiety.low: I barely think about it until the morning of the appointment.

Anxiety.situation: A friend said they needed to talk to you about something, but gave no details.
Anxiety.high: I spend the whole day anxious, imagining every bad thing it could possibly be.
Anxiety.low: I'm curious but not bothered — I'll find out when we talk.

Anxiety.situation: You're waiting for results from a job application you submitted.
Anxiety.high: I lie awake at night turning over every little thing I might have done wrong in the process.
Anxiety.low: I put it out of my mind and focus on other things until I hear back.

Anxiety.situation: You made a small mistake in a report at work that your manager already noticed.
Anxiety.high: I can't stop fixating on it — I keep imagining how it might affect how they see me.
Anxiety.low: I corrected it and let it go; everyone makes small errors sometimes.

Anxiety.situation: You're about to take a flight on a clear, calm day.
Anxiety.high: Even with perfect weather, I grip the armrest and mentally rehearse everything that could go wrong.
Anxiety.low: I settle in, put on headphones, and don't give the flight a second thought.

Dependence.situation: You receive some distressing news at work and still have hours left in your shift.
Dependence.high: I'd struggle to focus until I could call a close friend and talk it through with them.
Dependence.low: I'd feel shaken but push through — I can process it on my own when the day is done.

Dependence.situation: You're dealing with a difficult personal decision and feeling overwhelmed.
Dependence.high: I really need to talk to someone I trust before I can settle down enough to think clearly.
Dependence.low: I'd sit with it myself, weigh the options, and come to a conclusion without needing to loop anyone in.

Dependence.situation: A close relationship ends suddenly and you're left feeling upset.
Dependence.high: I'd immediately reach out to multiple friends — I can't handle that kind of pain alone.
Dependence.low: I'd give myself some quiet time to grieve and work through it before reaching out to anyone.

Dependence.situation: You're anxious about an upcoming medical procedure.
Dependence.high: I need someone to come with me and stay until it's over — I can't face it without support.
Dependence.low: I'd go on my own; it's uncomfortable but I don't need someone there to manage my nerves.

Dependence.situation: You made a mistake at work that's been weighing on you all evening.
Dependence.high: I keep texting my partner about it — I can't calm down until they reassure me it'll be okay.
Dependence.low: I'd acknowledge the error, think through what to fix, and let it go without needing anyone to talk me down.

Dependence.situation: You're traveling alone in an unfamiliar city and things keep going wrong.
Dependence.high: I'd call home right away — just hearing a familiar voice helps me cope when I feel lost.
Dependence.low: I'd sort it out myself; being on my own in a tough spot doesn't make me need to lean on anyone.

Sentimentality.situation: You watch a video of soldiers reuniting with their dogs after long deployments.
Sentimentality.high: I tear up almost immediately — something about that kind of reunion just gets me every time.
Sentimentality.low: It's a nice clip, but I don't really feel anything watching it — just people and their pets.

Sentimentality.situation: A colleague mentions offhandedly that today is the anniversary of their parent's death.
Sentimentality.high: I feel a wave of sadness for them and find myself thinking about it for the rest of the day.
Sentimentality.low: I say something brief and move on — I don't really dwell on other people's grief.

Sentimentality.situation: You're packing up your childhood bedroom for the last time before your parents sell the house.
Sentimentality.high: Holding old toys and photos, I feel genuinely emotional — it hits me that this chapter is really over.
Sentimentality.low: I just sort things into boxes efficiently; it's a building, and I don't get sentimental about places.

Sentimentality.situation: A friend sends you a playlist they made specifically for your birthday, with songs tied to shared memories.
Sentimentality.high: I'm moved that they put that care into it — listening to it, I feel a real swell of emotion.
Sentimentality.low: It's a thoughtful gesture, but a playlist is a playlist; I'm not going to get emotional over a list of songs.

Sentimentality.situation: At a wedding, the groom cries visibly during the vows.
Sentimentality.high: Seeing him cry makes me well up too — I can't help being swept into the feeling of the moment.
Sentimentality.low: I notice he's emotional, but it doesn't affect me personally; I stay dry-eyed through the whole thing.

Sentimentality.situation: You find a handwritten letter your grandmother wrote you years ago while cleaning out a drawer.
Sentimentality.high: Reading her words in her handwriting, I feel deeply moved — I have to sit with it for a while.
Sentimentality.low: I skim it, appreciate that she wrote it, and file it away without much emotional reaction.

Social Self-Esteem.situation: You walk into a party where you only know the host, who is busy greeting other guests.
Social Self-Esteem.high: I'd introduce myself to a few people nearby — I'm generally easy to talk to and people seem to like me.
Social Self-Esteem.low: I'd hover near the snacks hoping nobody notices how out of place I look.

Social Self-Esteem.situation: A colleague you admire invites you to join their lunch table with several people you haven't met.
Social Self-Esteem.high: I'd sit down and join the conversation — I hold my own pretty well in new groups.
Social Self-Esteem.low: I'd probably make an excuse to eat at my desk; I doubt I'd make a good impression on them.

Social Self-Esteem.situation: You receive a lukewarm reaction after sharing an idea in a group discussion.
Social Self-Esteem.high: I'd assume the idea just didn't land this time — it doesn't shake my sense of how I come across socially.
Social Self-Esteem.low: I'd spend the rest of the meeting convinced everyone now thinks I'm awkward or foolish.

Social Self-Esteem.situation: Someone you've met twice doesn't remember your name at a social event.
Social Self-Esteem.high: I'd laugh it off and re-introduce myself — no big deal, people forget names.
Social Self-Esteem.low: I'd take it as confirmation that I'm the kind of person who just doesn't leave an impression.

Social Self-Esteem.situation: You are invited to give a brief toast at a friend's birthday dinner with a dozen guests.
Social Self-Esteem.high: I'd feel comfortable doing it — I'm generally well-liked and people are usually receptive to what I say.
Social Self-Esteem.low: I'd decline if I could; I don't think people really want to hear from me in front of a crowd.

Social Self-Esteem.situation: After a group project wraps up, teammates are chatting and laughing without including you.
Social Self-Esteem.high: I'd step in and join — I probably just got left out by accident, it happens.
Social Self-Esteem.low: I'd quietly pack up and leave, assuming they've never found me that likable anyway.

Social Boldness.situation: You're at a professional networking event where you don't know anyone.
Social Boldness.high: I'd walk up to a small group mid-conversation and introduce myself without waiting for an opening.
Social Boldness.low: I'd hang near the snack table and hope someone approaches me first.

Social Boldness.situation: Your manager asks for volunteers to present the team's findings to senior leadership.
Social Boldness.high: I'd raise my hand immediately — I'm comfortable owning the room for that kind of thing.
Social Boldness.low: I'd stay quiet and hope someone else steps up; presenting to executives makes me clam up.

Social Boldness.situation: You're in a group discussion and you strongly disagree with what the most senior person just said.
Social Boldness.high: I'd speak up directly and lay out my counterargument, even if the room goes quiet.
Social Boldness.low: I'd probably just let it go — it's not worth the discomfort of challenging someone senior in public.

Social Boldness.situation: You arrive at a dinner party where you only know the host, who is busy in the kitchen.
Social Boldness.high: I'd introduce myself to whoever seems approachable and get a conversation going right away.
Social Boldness.low: I'd check my phone until the host is free — I find it awkward to approach strangers cold.

Social Boldness.situation: A speaker at a public lecture invites questions from the audience.
Social Boldness.high: I'd put my hand up without much hesitation if I had something worth asking.
Social Boldness.low: Even if I had a good question, I'd stay quiet rather than speak up in front of that many people.

Social Boldness.situation: You're assigned to lead a brainstorm with colleagues you've never worked with before.
Social Boldness.high: I'd take charge from the start, set the agenda out loud, and call on people to contribute.
Social Boldness.low: I'd wait to see how others want to run it and mostly follow their lead to avoid overstepping.

Sociability.situation: You arrive early to a work conference where you don't know anyone yet.
Sociability.high: I'd scan the room and introduce myself to a few people before the sessions begin.
Sociability.low: I'd find a corner seat and check my phone until the program starts.

Sociability.situation: A neighbor you've seen a few times is getting their mail at the same time as you.
Sociability.high: I'd stop and chat — ask how they're settling in, maybe learn their name.
Sociability.low: I'd give a quick nod and head back inside as soon as I can.

Sociability.situation: Your lunch break is free and the office kitchen has several coworkers chatting.
Sociability.high: I'd pull up a chair and join the conversation — it's a nice break from screens.
Sociability.low: I'd grab my food and eat at my desk; I'd rather have some quiet time.

Sociability.situation: You're waiting in a long line at the grocery store.
Sociability.high: I'd probably start up a lighthearted exchange with whoever is standing nearby.
Sociability.low: I'd keep to myself — there's nothing to talk about and I'd rather just wait.

Sociability.situation: You have an unexpectedly free Saturday with no plans.
Sociability.high: I'd text a bunch of people and try to put something social together for the afternoon.
Sociability.low: I'd enjoy the alone time — maybe read or go for a solo walk.

Sociability.situation: A work Slack channel has an off-topic thread where people are joking around.
Sociability.high: I'd jump in with a comment — I like staying connected with colleagues throughout the day.
Sociability.low: I'd mute the thread; that kind of chatter is mostly noise to me.

Liveliness.situation: It's Monday morning and your coworkers are sluggishly settling into the office.
Liveliness.high: I'd crack a joke and try to get everyone laughing before we dive in.
Liveliness.low: I'd quietly sit down and hope the morning passes quickly.

Liveliness.situation: You just finished a long, exhausting week of work.
Liveliness.high: I'd still feel a spark of energy and suggest grabbing drinks with friends tonight.
Liveliness.low: I'd feel completely drained and have no interest in doing anything social.

Liveliness.situation: A friend invites you to join an impromptu dance at a casual house party.
Liveliness.high: I'd jump up immediately — that sounds like exactly the kind of fun I need.
Liveliness.low: I'd wave them off and stay on the couch where it's comfortable.

Liveliness.situation: You're waiting in a long queue and notice someone standing nearby looking bored.
Liveliness.high: I'd strike up a cheerful conversation to pass the time for both of us.
Liveliness.low: I'd keep to myself and stare at my phone until it's my turn.

Liveliness.situation: Someone asks how you're doing on a perfectly ordinary Tuesday afternoon.
Liveliness.high: I'd say I'm great and mean it — ordinary days still feel pretty good to me.
Liveliness.low: I'd give a flat 'fine' and not really feel otherwise.

Liveliness.situation: Your team wins a small competition at a company event.
Liveliness.high: I'd cheer loudly and pump my fist — even small wins feel electric to me.
Liveliness.low: I'd manage a polite smile and move on without much feeling about it.

pairs.low: I'd hover near the snacks hoping nobody notices how out of place I look.

pairs.situation: Your team wins a small competition at a company event.
pairs.high: I'd cheer loudly and pump my fist — even small wins feel electric to me.

Forgiveness.situation: A close friend spread a rumor about you last year, then apologized sincerely. They now want to hang out again.
Forgiveness.high: I'd meet up — people make mistakes, and a genuine apology is enough for me to move forward.
Forgiveness.low: I'd be polite but keep them at a distance; once someone betrays your trust like that, it sticks.

Forgiveness.situation: A coworker took credit for your idea in a meeting but later admitted it privately and said sorry.
Forgiveness.high: I'd accept the apology and let it go — holding onto resentment just poisons my own mood.
Forgiveness.low: I'd say I accept it, but I'd make sure never to share work with them again and I'd remember it.

Forgiveness.situation: Your sibling forgot your birthday for the second time and sent a late, heartfelt apology.
Forgiveness.high: I'd tell them it hurt but that I forgive them — I don't want an old wound to color our whole relationship.
Forgiveness.low: I'd remind them of both times whenever birthdays come up; they need to feel the weight of consistently letting me down.

Forgiveness.situation: An acquaintance publicly mocked your career choice at a party, then reached out weeks later to apologize.
Forgiveness.high: I'd appreciate the apology and give them a fresh start — that moment doesn't have to define who they are to me.
Forgiveness.low: I'd acknowledge the message but inwardly write them off; what they said revealed something real about how they see me.

Forgiveness.situation: A former business partner broke an agreement that cost you money, later compensated you fully, and asked to collaborate again.
Forgiveness.high: I'd seriously consider it — they made it right, and I don't think past mistakes should permanently close every door.
Forgiveness.low: No chance; I'd be cordial if we crossed paths but I'd never trust them in any professional capacity again.

Forgiveness.situation: A neighbor who once reported you to the HOA over a minor issue now asks to borrow your ladder.
Forgiveness.high: Sure — that old dispute is water under the bridge, and lending a ladder costs me nothing.
Forgiveness.low: I'd tell them I'm not available to help; they weren't neighborly to me, so I don't feel obligated to be neighborly to them.

Gentleness.situation: A friend shows you a painting they worked hard on, and honestly it's technically quite poor.
Gentleness.high: I'd focus on the effort and find something genuine to appreciate about it.
Gentleness.low: I'd point out the obvious problems — they won't improve if nobody tells them the truth.

Gentleness.situation: A coworker gives a presentation that rambles and misses the main point.
Gentleness.high: I'd privately mention one area for improvement and leave it at that.
Gentleness.low: I'd bring up every weak spot in the debrief — that's how you raise the standard.

Gentleness.situation: Your partner has a habit of interrupting people mid-sentence during conversations.
Gentleness.high: I'd gently mention it once and let it go — everyone has little quirks.
Gentleness.low: I'd call it out every time it happens; it's rude and they need to know.

Gentleness.situation: A new employee submits a report with several avoidable formatting mistakes.
Gentleness.high: I'd show them the correct format and reassure them it's easy to fix.
Gentleness.low: I'd mark up every error and note that more care should have been taken the first time.

Gentleness.situation: Someone in your book club gives an interpretation of the novel that seems clearly off-base.
Gentleness.high: I'd gently offer a different reading without dismissing their take.
Gentleness.low: I'd tell them directly they missed the point — there's no use validating a wrong reading.

Gentleness.situation: A family member keeps mispronouncing a common word during conversation.
Gentleness.high: I'd let it go or mention it quietly once without making a big deal of it.
Gentleness.low: I'd correct them every time — there's no reason to let a mistake keep repeating.

Flexibility.situation: A colleague reviews your project plan and suggests a completely different approach than the one you've been developing for weeks.
Flexibility.high: I'd genuinely consider their approach — if it's better, I'm happy to scrap mine and start fresh.
Flexibility.low: I've already put weeks into this plan, and I'm not about to abandon it because someone else has a different idea.

Flexibility.situation: During a group debate, several people push back on your interpretation of the data.
Flexibility.high: Hearing multiple people disagree makes me wonder if I'm missing something — I'd revisit my interpretation.
Flexibility.low: I've looked at this data carefully, and a room full of opinions doesn't change what the numbers actually say.

Flexibility.situation: Your partner wants to take a different vacation route than the one you carefully planned.
Flexibility.high: Sure, let's try their route — I don't need to stick to my plan if they have reasons for preferring another way.
Flexibility.low: I already researched our route thoroughly, so I'd rather stick with what I planned unless there's a real problem with it.

Flexibility.situation: A friend argues that your long-held view on a political issue is based on outdated assumptions.
Flexibility.high: If they make a compelling case, I'm genuinely open to updating my view, even one I've held for years.
Flexibility.low: I've thought about this a lot longer than they have — one conversation isn't going to shift where I stand.

Flexibility.situation: You and a coworker disagree on how to organize a shared document, and they explain their reasoning.
Flexibility.high: Their reasoning makes sense, so I'd go with their system even though I had a different one in mind.
Flexibility.low: My system is more logical, and I'd keep pushing for it rather than just deferring to their preference.

Flexibility.situation: A family member challenges your dietary choices and shares evidence you hadn't seen before.
Flexibility.high: If the evidence is solid, I'd take it seriously and be willing to reconsider what I've been doing.
Flexibility.low: I know my own body and my own choices — I'm not changing my diet just because someone sends me an article.

Patience.situation: The person in front of you at the grocery store is slowly counting out exact change while a long line builds up behind you.
Patience.high: I wait quietly and remind myself they're just trying to be careful with their money.
Patience.low: I sigh loudly and mutter something so they can hear how annoying this is.

Patience.situation: A coworker keeps interrupting your explanation to ask questions you were about to answer.
Patience.high: I pause, answer each question patiently, and then continue without showing irritation.
Patience.low: I cut them off and tell them to just let me finish before jumping in.

Patience.situation: Your elderly relative tells the same story for the third time this visit.
Patience.high: I listen as if it's the first time and respond warmly to the parts they find meaningful.
Patience.low: I point out that they've already told me this and change the subject.

Patience.situation: A customer service rep keeps you on hold for fifteen minutes, then gives you the wrong information.
Patience.high: I calmly explain the issue again and ask if we can sort it out together.
Patience.low: I raise my voice and tell them this is completely unacceptable and a waste of my time.

Patience.situation: Someone at a meeting takes a very long, roundabout way to make a simple point.
Patience.high: I give them my full attention and let them finish without rushing them along.
Patience.low: I cut in and summarize their point for them so we can all move on.

Patience.situation: A friend is forty minutes late to meet you and only texts a casual apology when they arrive.
Patience.high: I let it go without making an issue of it and just enjoy the time together.
Patience.low: I tell them straight away that their lateness is disrespectful and I'm genuinely angry.

Organization.situation: You're packing for a week-long work trip the night before your flight.
Organization.high: I make a detailed checklist and lay everything out by category before folding it neatly into my suitcase.
Organization.low: I grab whatever seems right and stuff it in — I'll figure out what I forgot when I get there.

Organization.situation: A coworker asks to borrow a document you filed six months ago.
Organization.high: I pull it up in under a minute — I keep everything labeled and sorted in folders by date and project.
Organization.low: I spend twenty minutes digging through a pile of mixed files before admitting I can't find it.

Organization.situation: Your kitchen counter has accumulated mail, groceries, and random objects over the past week.
Organization.high: I clear and sort everything before it builds up further — clutter like that bothers me until it's dealt with.
Organization.low: I shove it all to one side to make room; it'll get sorted eventually when I need the space.

Organization.situation: You start a new personal project that involves tracking multiple tasks and deadlines.
Organization.high: I set up a structured system with clearly labeled sections, due dates, and progress notes from the start.
Organization.low: I jot things down on whatever scraps of paper are nearby and keep most of it in my head.

Organization.situation: You share a workspace with a colleague and each person maintains their own side of the desk.
Organization.high: My side has everything in a fixed place — I know exactly where each item belongs and put it back after use.
Organization.low: My side is usually buried under layers of stuff; I know where things are roughly, but nobody else would.

Organization.situation: After finishing a large cooking session, there are dishes, leftovers, and utensils spread across the kitchen.
Organization.high: I systematically put away leftovers, wipe surfaces, and wash everything before I let myself relax.
Organization.low: I cover the pots and leave the rest — I'm tired and it's easier to deal with tomorrow, or the day after.

Diligence.situation: You have a long report due in two weeks and no immediate deadline pressure today.
Diligence.high: I'd break it into daily targets and start drafting the first section this afternoon.
Diligence.low: I'd probably browse around, wait until I'm in the mood, and tackle it closer to the deadline.

Diligence.situation: You're halfway through a difficult online certification course and progress has slowed.
Diligence.high: I'd push through the hard sections — finishing what I started matters to me.
Diligence.low: I'd likely let it sit unfinished; if it's not clicking, it's probably not worth the effort.

Diligence.situation: A personal fitness goal you set three months ago is taking longer than expected.
Diligence.high: I'd reassess my approach and keep pushing — slow progress is still progress.
Diligence.low: I'd quietly drop it and tell myself I'll restart when conditions are better.

Diligence.situation: Your manager assigns you a tedious data-cleaning task with no recognition attached.
Diligence.high: I'd do it carefully and thoroughly — sloppy work reflects on me regardless of visibility.
Diligence.low: I'd do the bare minimum to get it off my plate and move on to something more interesting.

Diligence.situation: You're learning a new skill and hit a plateau where improvement feels invisible.
Diligence.high: I'd set a specific weekly practice target and track my progress until I break through.
Diligence.low: I'd probably coast at my current level; grinding through a plateau doesn't seem worth it.

Diligence.situation: You realize mid-project that achieving your original goal will require significantly more effort than planned.
Diligence.high: I'd raise my effort to match the goal — I don't like scaling back what I set out to do.
Diligence.low: I'd quietly lower the goal to match the effort I'm already comfortable putting in.

Perfectionism.situation: You've finished a report and have 20 minutes before it's due.
Perfectionism.high: I'd read through it again, checking every sentence for errors and tightening the wording.
Perfectionism.low: I'd send it off — it's good enough and I've already spent plenty of time on it.

Perfectionism.situation: You notice a minor formatting inconsistency in a slide deck you're about to present.
Perfectionism.high: I'd fix it immediately — mismatched fonts would bother me throughout the entire presentation.
Perfectionism.low: I'd leave it; the audience won't care and it doesn't affect the content at all.

Perfectionism.situation: A colleague glances at your draft and says it looks fine to them.
Perfectionism.high: I'd still go through it carefully myself — their standard of 'fine' might not match mine.
Perfectionism.low: That's all I need to hear; if someone else is satisfied, I'm done.

Perfectionism.situation: You're filling out a form that only you and one other person will ever see.
Perfectionism.high: I'd still complete every field carefully and double-check my entries before submitting.
Perfectionism.low: I'd fill it in quickly and move on — it's internal, so precision barely matters here.

Perfectionism.situation: You realize after sending an email that you misspelled one word in the third paragraph.
Perfectionism.high: I'd send a brief correction immediately — leaving a known error out there isn't acceptable to me.
Perfectionism.low: One typo in a long email is nothing; I'd never follow up over something so trivial.

Perfectionism.situation: You're finishing a personal creative project no one is evaluating or grading.
Perfectionism.high: I'd keep refining it until the execution actually matches what I envisioned, however long that takes.
Perfectionism.low: Once it's roughly what I had in mind, I'm happy to call it done and move on.

Prudence.situation: You're offered a great deal on a used car and the seller says you need to decide today.
Prudence.high: I'd ask for a day to have a mechanic inspect it and review the vehicle history before committing.
Prudence.low: It feels right, so I'd sign the paperwork on the spot and sort out any issues later.

Prudence.situation: You feel a strong urge to quit your job after a frustrating meeting with your boss.
Prudence.high: I'd wait a few days before doing anything, and think through my financial situation and options carefully.
Prudence.low: I'd walk out that afternoon — staying somewhere that makes me feel that way isn't worth it.

Prudence.situation: You're at a casino and you're up significantly from your starting amount.
Prudence.high: I'd pocket the winnings and stop — I came with a budget and I've already done better than expected.
Prudence.low: I'd keep playing while I'm on a hot streak; you have to ride the feeling when it's there.

Prudence.situation: A friend pitches you an investment opportunity and asks for your answer by end of day.
Prudence.high: I'd tell them I need at least a week to research the details before putting any money in.
Prudence.low: If my gut says it sounds good, I'd wire the money that evening and work out the details after.

Prudence.situation: You're drafting an angry email to a colleague who publicly undermined you.
Prudence.high: I'd write the email, then sit on it overnight and decide whether sending it is really the right move.
Prudence.low: I'd hit send immediately — they need to hear it and I'm not going to second-guess myself.

Prudence.situation: You're browsing online and find an expensive gadget you hadn't planned to buy.
Prudence.high: I'd add it to a wishlist and revisit it in two weeks to see if I still want it that badly.
Prudence.low: I'd buy it right away — if I'm excited enough to notice it, that's reason enough to get it.

pairs.situation: You're packing for a week-long work trip the night before your flight.
pairs.high: I make a detailed checklist and lay everything out by category before folding it neatly into my suitcase.
pairs.low: I grab whatever seems right and stuff it in — I'll figure out what I forgot when I get there.

pairs.situation: A coworker asks to borrow a document you filed six months ago.
pairs.high: I pull it up in under a minute — I keep everything labeled and sorted in folders by date and project.
pairs.low: I spend twenty minutes digging through a pile of mixed files before admitting I can't find it.

pairs.situation: Your kitchen counter has accumulated mail, groceries, and random objects over the past week.
pairs.high: I clear and sort everything before it builds up further — clutter like that bothers me until it's dealt with.
pairs.low: I shove it all to one side to make room; it'll get sorted eventually when I need the space.

pairs.situation: You start a new personal project that involves tracking multiple tasks and deadlines.
pairs.high: I set up a structured system with clearly labeled sections, due dates, and progress notes from the start.
pairs.low: I jot things down on whatever scraps of paper are nearby and keep most of it in my head.

pairs.situation: You share a workspace with a colleague and each person maintains their own side of the desk.
pairs.high: My side has everything in a fixed place — I know exactly where each item belongs and put it back after use.
pairs.low: My side is usually buried under layers of stuff; I know where things are roughly, but nobody else would.

pairs.situation: After finishing a large cooking session, there are dishes, leftovers, and utensils spread across the kitchen.
pairs.high: I systematically put away leftovers, wipe surfaces, and wash everything before I let myself relax.
pairs.low: I cover the pots and leave the rest — I'm tired and it's easier to deal with tomorrow, or the day after.

pairs.situation: You have a long report due in two weeks and no immediate deadline pressure today.
pairs.high: I'd break it into daily targets and start drafting the first section this afternoon.
pairs.low: I'd probably browse around, wait until I'm in the mood, and tackle it closer to the deadline.

pairs.situation: You're halfway through a difficult online certification course and progress has slowed.
pairs.high: I'd push through the hard sections — finishing what I started matters to me.
pairs.low: I'd likely let it sit unfinished; if it's not clicking, it's probably not worth the effort.

pairs.situation: A personal fitness goal you set three months ago is taking longer than expected.
pairs.high: I'd reassess my approach and keep pushing — slow progress is still progress.
pairs.low: I'd quietly drop it and tell myself I'll restart when conditions are better.

O.name: Openness to Experience
O.high_descriptor: creative, curious, unconventional, and aesthetically sensitive

Aesthetic Appreciation.situation: A friend invites you to an evening of chamber music at a small local venue.
Aesthetic Appreciation.high: I'd genuinely look forward to it — there's something moving about hearing live strings in an intimate space.
Aesthetic Appreciation.low: Honestly, sitting through that sounds tedious; I'd rather find a reason to skip it.

Aesthetic Appreciation.situation: You pass an art museum with free admission on your afternoon off.
Aesthetic Appreciation.high: I'd wander in and lose track of time studying the paintings.
Aesthetic Appreciation.low: I'd walk right past — staring at pictures on walls has never done anything for me.

Aesthetic Appreciation.situation: A colleague shares a poem they found deeply beautiful and reads it aloud to you.
Aesthetic Appreciation.high: I'd listen carefully and ask them what images struck them most.
Aesthetic Appreciation.low: I'd nod politely but privately think it's just words arranged on a page.

Aesthetic Appreciation.situation: You're choosing between a plain functional coffee mug and a handcrafted ceramic one at the same price.
Aesthetic Appreciation.high: I'd pick the ceramic one — the texture and irregular glaze make every morning feel a little richer.
Aesthetic Appreciation.low: I'd grab the plain one; fancy design on a mug seems like a pointless indulgence.

Aesthetic Appreciation.situation: An architectural walking tour of your city is offered on a free Saturday.
Aesthetic Appreciation.high: I'd sign up immediately — I love noticing details in buildings I usually walk past without seeing.
Aesthetic Appreciation.low: It's not my idea of a good time; old buildings are just old buildings to me.

Aesthetic Appreciation.situation: A documentary about the making of a celebrated symphony is on television tonight.
Aesthetic Appreciation.high: I'd settle in to watch it — I find the creative process behind great music genuinely fascinating.
Aesthetic Appreciation.low: I'd change the channel; I can't imagine caring how a symphony got written.

Inquisitiveness.situation: A colleague mentions an obscure economic theory you've never heard of during lunch.
Inquisitiveness.high: I'd ask them to explain it and look it up later to read more about it.
Inquisitiveness.low: I'd nod along and steer the conversation back to something more familiar.

Inquisitiveness.situation: You finish a novel set in feudal Japan and notice you know nothing about that era.
Inquisitiveness.high: I'd go down a rabbit hole reading about the shogunate, samurai culture, and the period's politics.
Inquisitiveness.low: I'd move on to the next novel — I read fiction to relax, not to study history.

Inquisitiveness.situation: A documentary about deep-sea biology starts playing on TV while you're flipping channels.
Inquisitiveness.high: I'd stop and watch — creatures that live miles underwater are genuinely fascinating to me.
Inquisitiveness.low: I'd keep flipping — I'm not really into nature documentaries.

Inquisitiveness.situation: Someone at a party starts explaining how black holes emit Hawking radiation.
Inquisitiveness.high: I'd lean in and ask follow-up questions — I love understanding how the universe actually works.
Inquisitiveness.low: I'd listen politely for a minute, then excuse myself to refill my drink.

Inquisitiveness.situation: You stumble across a long Wikipedia article about the Byzantine Empire.
Inquisitiveness.high: I'd read the whole thing and follow the links — I find that kind of historical depth captivating.
Inquisitiveness.low: I'd skim the first paragraph and close the tab — it's not something I need to know.

Inquisitiveness.situation: A friend invites you to a public lecture on the philosophy of language.
Inquisitiveness.high: I'd go eagerly — the idea of dissecting how language shapes thought sounds genuinely interesting.
Inquisitiveness.low: I'd pass — sitting through an academic lecture on a topic like that sounds pretty tedious.

Creativity.situation: Your manager asks how you'd like to present the quarterly results to the team.
Creativity.high: I'd pitch an interactive visual story with charts that animate as we walk through the narrative.
Creativity.low: I'd use the standard slide deck template we always use — it covers everything cleanly.

Creativity.situation: You have a free Saturday afternoon with no plans.
Creativity.high: I'd start sketching out a short story idea that's been rattling around in my head.
Creativity.low: I'd rewatch a favorite show or catch up on sleep — nothing too demanding.

Creativity.situation: A friend asks for help planning an unusual birthday party for their child.
Creativity.high: I'd immediately start brainstorming a theme from scratch — maybe a detective mystery they'd actually solve.
Creativity.low: I'd look up popular kids' party packages and pick whichever one had the best reviews.

Creativity.situation: You need to write a short bio for a conference program.
Creativity.high: I'd write it as a brief narrative with an unexpected opening line to make it stand out.
Creativity.low: I'd list my job title, years of experience, and key credentials in straightforward sentences.

Creativity.situation: Your team is stuck on a product problem with no obvious solution.
Creativity.high: I'd push for a wild brainstorm session where no idea is off-limits, even absurd ones.
Creativity.low: I'd go back to what competitors have done and adapt whichever approach worked best for them.

Creativity.situation: You're decorating a new apartment on a limited budget.
Creativity.high: I'd repurpose old materials and design a few pieces myself to give the space a distinctive look.
Creativity.low: I'd pick a popular style from a home décor site and order whatever fits the budget.

Unconventionality.situation: A colleague pitches an unusual theory about why your industry's standard practices are all wrong.
Unconventionality.high: I'd lean in and ask them to walk me through it — even fringe theories sometimes crack something open.
Unconventionality.low: I'd nod politely but privately dismiss it — we follow standard practices for good reasons.

Unconventionality.situation: You're browsing a bookstore and notice a book arguing that most of modern science rests on flawed assumptions.
Unconventionality.high: I'd flip it open right there — I love when someone makes a serious case against received wisdom.
Unconventionality.low: I'd put it back; that kind of contrarianism usually just muddies the waters without adding anything real.

Unconventionality.situation: A friend invites you to a lecture by a philosopher who rejects the concept of personal identity entirely.
Unconventionality.high: I'd definitely go — ideas that unsettle your basic assumptions are the most interesting ones.
Unconventionality.low: I'd pass; I don't really see the point of entertaining ideas that have no practical grounding.

Unconventionality.situation: At a dinner party, someone starts arguing that mainstream economic thinking is fundamentally misguided.
Unconventionality.high: I'd encourage them to keep going — I find heterodox economic views genuinely worth taking seriously.
Unconventionality.low: I'd steer the conversation elsewhere; those arguments rarely go anywhere useful.

Unconventionality.situation: You hear about an artist whose work deliberately violates every convention of their medium.
Unconventionality.high: That immediately makes me want to see it — breaking conventions is often where the interesting stuff lives.
Unconventionality.low: It sounds like a gimmick to me; mastering conventions matters before you can meaningfully break them.

Unconventionality.situation: A new acquaintance tells you they hold several beliefs that go sharply against mainstream consensus.
Unconventionality.high: I'd be curious about how they got there — people with unconventional views often have interesting reasoning.
Unconventionality.low: I'd be a bit wary — that's usually a sign someone hasn't thought things through carefully.
